message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The novel coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, is responsible for a world-wide pandemic. While the medical community understands the mode of viral transmission, less is known about how long viral shedding occurs once viral symptoms have resolved.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine how long the SARS-CoV-2 remains detectable following self-reporting of viral symptom resolution.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A cohort of 86 previously SARS-CoV-2 positive patients were re-tested for proof of viral recovery by nasal swab and nucleic acid amplification less than 28 days after self-reported symptom resolution. This study was approved by the University of Wisconsin Institutional Review Board.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A tertiary care center in a mid-size city utilizing a drive-through SAR-CoV-2 testing center.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>86 previously confirmed SARS-CoV-2 positive individuals less than 28 days after self-reported resolution of symptoms evaluated as potential donors for COVID-19 convalescent plasma.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants underwent nasopharyngeal sampling and subsequent nucleic acid amplification for SARS-CoV-2 genes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 RNA in nasopharyngeal secretions detected by rtPCR.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    11/86 (13%) previously confirmed SARS-CoV-2 subjects were still positive at a median of 19 days (range 12-24 days) after symptom resolution. Older patients were more likely to be test-positive, and older positive patients had lower rtPCR C
                    <jats:sub>T</jats:sub>
                    values. Test-positive patients were not significantly different from test-negative patients with respect to days since symptom recovery.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Resolution</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These results underscore the necessity of testing COVID-19 convalescent plasma donors less than 28 days after symptom resolution and suggests that COVID-19 positive patients may need to remain in quarantine beyond the recommended two weeks following “recovery.”</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Trial Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>n/a</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Prolonged viral RNA shedding after COVID-19 symptom resolution in older convalescent plasma donors
